summary: The team secured 1st place by building large ensembles of CNN, GRU, LSTM, and 3D CNN models tailored for IMU, thermal, and time-of-flight sensor data. Key drivers included rigorous sequence cleaning, extensive IMU feature engineering (jerks, correlations, gravity-removed accelerations), and targeted augmentations like mixup and time shift/stretch. They validated using a group-by-subject K-fold strategy, trained models with AdamW/Adam and cosine annealing schedulers, and blended logits across IMU-only, all-features, and TOF-only subsets. Post-processing adjusted predictions to respect class and orientation distribution constraints, while slightly increasing inference sequence length to decorrelate models.
extensive_summary:
## Solution Overview
The team won 1st place by constructing large ensembles of deep learning models specifically adapted for multi-modal sensor data (IMU, THM, TOF). The pipeline emphasized rigorous data cleaning, extensive IMU feature engineering, targeted augmentations, and a group-by-subject K-fold validation strategy. Final predictions were blended via a weighted sum of logits and adjusted post-hoc to respect class and orientation distribution constraints.

## Data Processing & Feature Engineering
Raw sequences were cleaned by removing subjects who wore devices incorrectly and filtering out sequences with low gesture ratios or completely missing TOF data. TOF values of -1 were replaced with 500 to encode large distances, and NaNs were filled using `seq.ffill().bfill().fillna(0)`. Sequences were padded or cropped to the 94th percentile (~120 max length). Handedness was normalized by flipping IMU axes and reflecting quaternion signs. While some models used a robust scaler, the author found that applying no scalers yielded the largest early-stage improvement. IMU feature engineering was extensive, producing 35 features including accelerations, jerks, correlations, gravity-removed accelerations, and their squared variants.

## Model Architectures & Training
Multiple architectures were explored across modalities. Devin's part utilized CNN-GRU hybrids with separate stems, pure CNNs with residual blocks and expanding kernels (5, 9, 13), dense GRUs, self-excitation models, and a 3D CNN for TOF data. Ogurtsov's part focused on IMU-only and all-features models using LSTM, attention, or CNN-based time dimension processing, with a key innovation of independently processing feature groups in early layers. zyz's part used simple RNN and CNN1D combinations. Training ran for 60-220 epochs with batch size 64, using `torch.optim.Adam` or `AdamW` with learning rates between `4e-4` and `5e-4`, weight decay `3e-4`, and schedulers like `CosineAnnealingWarmRestarts` or `LinearLR`.

## Augmentation & Ensemble Strategy
Augmentations included up/down-scaling, random noise, masking, mixup (`MIXUP_ALPHA = 0.4`), and time shift/stretch transforms. The author noted that picking the right augmentation probability, strength, and order was difficult. The final submission blended IMU-only, all-features, and TOF-only models as a weighted sum of logits. Post-processing accounted for limited class and orientation samples per subject. During inference, sequence length was increased by 5 to decorrelate models, and the best fold results across 3 runs were selected.
competition_flow: Raw sensor data (IMU, THM, TOF) was cleaned, normalized, and augmented, then fed into large ensembles of CNN, GRU, LSTM, and 3D CNN models trained with group-by-subject K-fold validation; logits were blended and post-processed to respect class/orientation constraints before submission.
data_processing: Removed subjects who wore devices incorrectly and filtered sequences with low gesture ratios or completely missing TOF data
Replaced TOF -1 values with 500 to encode large distances
Padded/cropped sequences to the 94th percentile (~120 max length)
Normalized handedness by flipping IMU axes and reflecting quaternion signs
Applied robust scaler in some models but found no scalers yielded better early-stage improvement
features_engineering: 35 IMU features including accelerations, jerks, correlations, gravity-removed accelerations, and their squared variants
Temporal differences of TOF features and thermal data
Independent processing of feature groups in early network layers
cv_strategy: CV5 with group by subject K-fold, using 5 models on inference.
ensembling: Large ensembles of IMU-only, all-features, and TOF-only models were blended as a weighted sum of logits, with post-processing applied to account for limited class and orientation samples per subject.
insights: Normalizing handedness by flipping IMU axes and reflecting quaternion signs is critical for cross-subject generalization.
Independently processing feature groups in early network layers significantly improves performance.
Increasing sequence length by 5 during inference effectively decorrelates models without hurting CV scores.
Mixup and time-based augmentations require careful tuning of probability and strength to be effective.
critical_findings: Using no scalers provided the largest improvement at the early training stage compared to using them.
A bug in cross-attention (shared weights for 3 different attentions) surprisingly worked well and was kept.
Class weights are meaningless for mixup samples but can still serve as effective regularization.
-1 in TOF data encodes large distance and should be replaced with 500 rather than treated as missing.
what_did_not_work: dense GRU scored lower but helped slightly in ensemble
some self excitation models did not combine well
picking the right augmentation probability, strength, and order was difficult
